method_atom: An operator-algebraic formalism that generalizes the theory of measurement: composite alternatives are represented as entangled elements of a lattice, acted on by a noncommutative algebra of positive operators (a probability-operator measure) that plays the role of the local-observable algebra. Because the alternatives are composite and the operators entangle, cross (interference) terms appear that make the operations noncommutative and the outcome probabilities non-additive, giving a self-consistent framework that reproduces deviations from the classical additive-utility/expected-value calculus.
object_atom: Human decision makers choosing among prospects, and the catalogue of paradoxes that arise when classical utility theory is applied to real human choice; the same formalism is also offered for atomic multimode registers and molecular high-spin (condensed-matter) information-processing systems.
brief_reason: The transferable part is the entangled-lattice-plus-noncommutative-positive-operator machinery whose interference cross-terms yield non-additive probabilities; the domain-specific part is human decision making (and its paradoxes) as the subject the machinery is applied to.
